title: "Income and Substitution Effects"
date: "2023-07-05"
categories: [microeconomics]
format:
  html: 
    code-fold: false
---

In the previous notes, we have discussed about the nuts and bolts of consumer choice. At the very end, we talked about Lagrangian method to solve utility maximization problem. Solving the problem yields a set of individual function:
$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{1}^{*} &= D_{1}(p_{1}, p_{2}, \cdots, p_{n},I)\\
X_{2}^{*} &= D_{2}(p_{1}, p_{2}, \cdots, p_{n},I)\\
 \vdots\\
X_{n}^{*} &= D_{n}(p_{1}, p_{2}, \cdots, p_{n},I)
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $d_{k}$ denotes an individual's demand function of good $k$, $p_{k}$ denotes the price of good $k$, and $I$ denotes the individual's income.

## Demand Functions

::: {.cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Define the function
def f(x):
    return x**2

# Generate x values
x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 100)

# Generate y values
y = f(x)

# Create the plot
plt.plot(x, y, 'r-', linewidth=2)

# Add labels and title
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('f(x)')
plt.title('Convex Function')

# Set axis limits
plt.xlim([-2, 2])
plt.ylim([0, 4])

# Display the plot
plt.show()
```
